title: Developer tools should feed the agent you already trust
tldr: The strongest AI product strategy may be exposing secure APIs, documentation, skills, and MCP tools to users' existing coding agents instead of building another weaker in-app assistant.
summary:
AI-assisted engineering is splitting into two uncomfortable camps: developers who automate nearly everything, and maintainers who absorb the cost of sloppy machine-generated code. The useful response is not choosing automation or craftsmanship as identities; it is designing workflows where agents remove mechanical work while engineers retain review, system understanding, and accountability.

For developer-tool companies, that means reconsidering the obligatory built-in assistant. Power users already have an agent whose model, local repository access, memory, and instructions they have tuned. A product-specific assistant usually cannot match that environment, and serving the strongest models to every customer is expensive. Building yet another agent can leave a company funding a watered-down competitor to the tool its customers already prefer.

The alternative is to make the product **agent-ready**: publish parseable documentation, expose a stable API, provide reusable skills, and ship an authenticated MCP server. The same primitives can power the vendor's own interface while also letting Claude Code, Codex, or another trusted agent operate the product. Cloudflare's approach illustrates the compression benefit: its API can be reached through one MCP server with roughly 1,000 tokens of tool context instead of exposing a million-plus tokens of endpoint definitions at once.

Security and economics still decide whether this becomes a business rather than a commodity utility. Authentication must bound access, destructive operations need deliberate controls, and proprietary data or differentiated execution remains the moat. Usage-based pricing aligns better with agents that invoke capabilities programmatically than charging customers for an assistant they may ignore.

The practical workflow tip applies the same principle to code review. Claude Code artifacts can turn a real diff, its surrounding import graph, the reasons behind each change, and test evidence into a shareable PR walkthrough. The artifact stays private until shared and is visible only to authenticated organization members. Let the agent produce the mechanical explanation, but keep the reviewer responsible for checking that the reasoning and tests actually support the change.
keyPoints: Heavy AI use creates leverage only when engineers still own review quality and system accountability.
Built-in assistants compete with coding agents that already have better models, local context, and user trust.
Agent-ready products expose secure docs, APIs, skills, and MCP servers that any trusted agent can consume.
Authentication, controlled destructive actions, proprietary data, and usage-based pricing keep agent integrations defensible.
PR walkthrough artifacts can explain diffs and tests, but they do not transfer review responsibility to the agent.

quiz.question: Why can an in-app agent be unattractive to power users?
options: It always has too much local access
It often lacks the model, repository context, and tuning of their existing agent
quiz.explanation: Power users have already configured agents around their local code and preferred models, which a siloed assistant struggles to match.

quiz.question: What is the proposed alternative to building another standalone assistant?
options: Expose secure documentation, APIs, skills, and MCP tools
Put every API endpoint into the prompt
quiz.explanation: Agent-ready primitives let customers keep their trusted agent while integrating the product's capabilities.

quiz.question: What strategic constraint remains essential for an MCP-enabled product?
quiz.explanation: Secure access and proprietary value prevent the integration from becoming an unsafe commodity utility.

quiz.question: What can a Claude Code PR artifact include?
options: The diff, reasoning, surrounding code, and test evidence
An automatic approval from every reviewer
quiz.explanation: The artifact builds a walkthrough from the actual change and its context, while review responsibility remains human.
